title: The chimera of universal coverage in a large, diverse country
subtitle: Our findings suggest that policies intended to subsidize health insurance of higher income groups, for example, the enhanced premium subsidies, are far less efficient than policies intended to further expand public insurance to low-income groups, for example, in non-expansion states. That is from a new NBER working paper by Anuj Gangopadhyaya & Robert Kaestner. The post The chimera of universal coverage in a large, diverse country appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION .
contentHtml:
Our findings suggest that policies intended to subsidize health insurance of higher income groups, for example, the enhanced premium subsidies, are far less efficient than policies intended to further expand public insurance to low-income groups, for example, in non-expansion states.

That is from a new NBER working paper by

The post The chimera of universal coverage in a large, diverse country appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.
